title: Coil-and-pinch handled tankard in air-dry clay
subtitle: A tall mug-tankard form stacked from coils, smoothed by hand, and finished with a strap handle
excerpt: A tall tankard built by stacking coils on a slab base, then smoothing the inside and outside walls by pinching the coils together. The form holds 350 ml when full but is decorative only: air-dry clay is porous and not waterproof. A wide strap handle is attached at the rim and lower wall.
sourceNotes: Coil-and-pinch construction is documented across pre-wheel ceramic traditions and remains the standard hand-building method for tall cylindrical forms. V&A open-access collection for the historical tankard reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or along a board. The building unit of coil-pot construction.
Scratching the joining surfaces of two pieces of clay and brushing them with slurry before pressing together. The standard join for any clay-to-clay attachment that must hold.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. The surface feels firm, like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale, uniform in colour, and fragile. For air-dry clay, bone-dry is the finished structural state.

content.text: A tankard is a tall straight-sided mug, usually wider in capacity than an everyday cup. This version stacks rolled coils on a slab base, then pinches the coil seams together inside and out to make a single continuous wall. No kiln is required: the clay is air-dry, and the finished tankard sets at room temperature over two to three days. The piece is decorative. Air-dry clay is porous and not suitable for liquids, so the tankard works as a brush pot, a utensil holder, or a display object.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay (air-dry) gives stronger joins at the same weight

items.name: Wooden rib
items.substitution: A blunt butter knife works for smoothing

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the full 500 g of clay for three or four minutes on the board. The wedge homogenises the moisture and removes air pockets. Cut off 100 g for the base and handle and reserve the remaining 400 g under a plastic bag so it does not dry while you work.

Set up a small dish of slip for the joining work: a tablespoon of dry clay offcuts mixed with enough water in a saucer to reach a yoghurt consistency. Keep a damp sponge and a small bowl of water within reach. The work goes faster if everything is in arm's reach before the first coil is rolled.
Building the tankard
1. Roll the base

From the 100 g reserve, take 60 g and flatten it with the heel of the hand into a disc about 8 mm thick. Trim to a circle 80 mm across using the fettling knife and a jar lid as a template. This is the base. Set it on the wooden board. Reserve the remaining 40 g for the handle.
2. Roll the first coil

Pinch off 50 g from the reserved 400 g. Roll it between the palms first to a stubby sausage, then on the board with both hands flat, fingers spread, working from the centre outward. The finished coil should be a uniform 12 mm thick and long enough to wrap the base in a single loop, about 260 mm long.
3. Attach the first coil with score and slip

Score the top edge of the base disc and the underside of the coil with cross-hatched needle marks 1-2 mm deep. Brush both with slip (clay thinned with water to a yoghurt consistency, mixed in a small dish from offcuts). Press the coil down onto the base, all the way round, joining the coil ends with a smooth diagonal cut so the seam disappears into the wall. This
score-and-slip
join is the standard for any clay-to-clay attachment.
4. Smooth the join from inside and outside

With a fingertip or the rounded end of the wooden rib, drag the coil clay downward onto the base on the inside of the tankard. The clay should flow into the join until no seam is visible. Repeat on the outside. This pinching-down move is what turns a stack of coils into a continuous wall.
5. Add the next coils
Roll the next
coil

to the same 12 mm thickness. Score, slip, lay it on top of the previous coil, and smooth in both directions. Continue, one coil at a time, until the wall reaches about 110 mm in height. Six coils is typical at 50 g each. Aim for a finished wall thickness of 8 mm throughout: thicker than a pinch pot because the tankard is taller and needs the wall to support the weight above it.
6. Refine the inside and outside

Once the wall is built, smooth the inside with a wooden rib using vertical strokes. The rib consolidates the clay and removes any remaining coil texture. On the outside, smooth with the rib if a plain finish is wanted, or leave the soft coil ridges visible for a more hand-built look. Check the wall is upright by eye, holding the board at eye level and turning it. Gently press in or out where the wall has leaned.
7. Level the rim

The top rim is usually uneven after building. Lay the fettling knife flat across the rim and trim down to a single level by cutting with a gentle sawing motion. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger and round it slightly with a damp sponge. A square-cut rim feels harsher in the hand than a softly rounded one.
Making and attaching the handle
Wait until the tankard wall is firm enough to take the weight of a handle without distorting. This is the early
leather-hard
stage: the clay is darker than its dry colour, holds shape under light pressure, and feels cool to the touch. Around two hours of open-air drying, depending on room conditions.

Take the reserved 40 g of clay and roll it into a flat strap on the board with the rolling pin, about 70 mm long, 18 mm wide, and 6 mm thick. Bend it into a soft loop and check the size against the tankard: the handle should sit comfortably under two fingers without crowding the wall.

Score the two ends of the handle and the two spots on the tankard wall where it will attach: one just below the rim, the other about 60 mm down. Brush both with slip. Press the top end of the handle to the upper attachment point first, then bend the loop and press the lower end on. With a fingertip, blend the join inward from the handle onto the wall, leaving a small thumb-print fillet for strength rather than scraping the join flat. The fillet is the load-bearing reinforcement.
Drying

Cover the tankard loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first 6 hours after the handle is on. This evens the drying speed between the thick handle joins and the thinner wall, which prevents the joins from cracking. Remove the plastic and let the piece dry openly to
bone-dry
over two to three days. Drying time depends on wall thickness, room humidity, and the size of the handle joins.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand any rough spots with 220-grit sandpaper, working over a sheet of newspaper to catch the dust. Wipe with a damp sponge to lift the surface dust. Paint, draw on, or leave plain. Seal with two thin coats of PVA or acrylic varnish so the surface is wipeable. The first coat soaks in; the second forms the protective film.
Care

Air-dry clay is porous and not waterproof. The sealed tankard works for dry contents: a brush jar at the desk, a holder for cooking utensils on the counter, dried flowers. Do not fill with liquid, do not put in the dishwasher, do not use as a drinking vessel.

Wipe clean with a damp (not wet) cloth. Avoid leaving wet items inside; water sitting in the base softens the air-dry clay over time and the base can collapse. If the tankard is going to be on display in a sunny room, consider a UV-resistant acrylic varnish over the standard sealer to slow any colour shift in any paint or wash applied to the surface.
Variations
Build the same form in paper clay for stronger handle joins and a forgiving green stage.
Leave the outside coils visible and unpinched for a deliberate ridged texture, working as a decorative feature.
Scale down to 250 g of clay for a small espresso-cup-sized tankard with a finger-loop handle.

items.symptom: Wall leans as it grows taller
items.cause: Coils added in fast succession while the wall below is still soft
items.fix: Stop after every two or three coils and let the wall firm for 30-45 minutes before continuing

items.symptom: Coil seams crack open during drying
items.cause: Coils joined without scoring or slip, or wall pinched only on the outside
items.fix: On a new piece, score and slip every join and smooth from both sides; for a small crack already formed, work fresh slip into it and dry slowly under plastic

items.symptom: Handle pops off as it dries
items.cause: Score-and-slip step skipped, or the wall and the handle were at different dryness levels when joined
items.fix: Both pieces should be at the same early leather-hard stage; score firmly, slip generously, and leave a small fillet of clay at the join for strength

items.cause: Base disc dried faster than the walls and shrank against them
items.fix: Cover with plastic loosely for the first day so the whole piece dries at one speed
